title: Cognitive Bias Detective
creator: Dan McCreary
subject: Ecology
description: Gamified simulation for detecting cognitive biases and logical fallacies in environmental arguments across three difficulty levels

learningObjectives: Detect cognitive biases and logical fallacies in environmental arguments
Explain how each bias type distorts reasoning about ecological issues
Distinguish between valid scientific arguments and flawed reasoning
Apply critical thinking skills to evaluate real-world environmental media
prerequisites: Familiarity with argument and evidence concepts
Understanding of the difference between opinion and scientific evidence

pedagogical.teachingStrategy: Gamified critical thinking practice with progressive difficulty
keyQuestions: Which cognitive biases are most common in environmental media?
How can you distinguish between a valid appeal to scientific consensus and an appeal to authority?
Why is false balance particularly harmful in science reporting?
How do your own biases affect how you interpret environmental claims?
What strategies can you use to evaluate environmental claims more objectively?
commonMisconceptions: Natural always means safe or better (the appeal to nature fallacy)
Giving equal time to both sides is always fair (false balance ignores scientific consensus)
If an argument attacks a person it must be wrong -- but ad hominem goes both ways; attacking the person does not address the argument
assessmentOpportunities: Have students identify biases in real environmental news articles
Ask students to rewrite a biased claim as a fair, evidence-based argument
Challenge students to create their own biased claim cards for classmates to detect
